AN ACT to deal with the retention of evidence in criminal matters, the use of samples as evidence and for related matters.
Commencement [27 February 1999]

1.   Short title
This Act may be cited as the Administration of Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act.

2.   Interpretation
In this Act—
“
agent
” means a person authorised in writing by the accused to act on behalf of the accused;
“
attorney-at-law
” includes a barrister or solicitor;
“
senior police officer
” means a police officer of the rank of Inspector and above.

3.   Use of photograph as evidence
(1)   Subject to subsections (2) to (7), a photograph of a scene or of anything seized by the police for the purposes of a criminal investigation shall be admitted for use as evidence in a criminal trial.
(2)   Subject to subsection (8), a photograph of goods or moveable property shall not be admitted in evidence unless—
(a)     the photograph was taken before a senior police officer in the presence of the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent; or
(b)     a copy of the photograph is signed by the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent in the presence of a senior police officer acknowledging that the photograph is a true likeness of the thing photographed.
(3)   A copy of any photograph that is taken under subsection (2) shall be served on the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent personally or by registered post within 21 days of its being taken and the person executing the service shall make an endorsement of service on the copy of the photograph kept by the police.
(4)   The accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent may, not later than 21 days after the receipt of the copy of the photograph, object by notice in writing addressed to the Commissioner of Police to the photograph being used as evidence and the notice of objection shall state the reasons for the objection.
(5)   Where a photograph is offered as evidence in a criminal trial, and no notice of objection has been issued, the production of negatives shall not be required.
(6)   Where goods or moveable property have been seized by the police for the purposes of a criminal investigation, and no objection has been made under subsection (4), the goods or moveable property shall not be retained for use as evidence once photographs have been taken and the Commissioner of Police shall cause the goods and moveable property to be returned to the owner.
(7)   Where it appears that the goods or property referred to in subsection (6) are owned by a person other than the accused, and an objection has been made under subsection (4), the Commissioner of Police shall return the goods to the owner provided that the owner signs the undertaking set out in Schedule 1.
(8)   Where a photograph was taken in the absence of the accused, the accused's attorney-at-law or agent or a copy of the photograph was not signed by the accused, his or her attorney-at-law or his or her agent, the person who took the photograph and the senior police officer shall make a statement certifying that the photograph was taken in the absence of the accused, his or her attorney-at-law or his or her agent, or that a copy of the photograph was not signed by the accused, the accused's attorney-at-law or agent and the reason for its not being signed.

4.   Use of samples as evidence
(1)   A sample of any controlled drug within the meaning of section 4 of the Drugs (Prevention of Misuse) Act or any other substance which has been seized by the police for the purposes of a criminal investigation shall be admitted as evidence in a criminal trial if, in the presence of the accused, his or her attorney-at-law or agent—
(a)     the sample is taken from the bulk of the substance;
(b)     the sample and the substance from which it is taken are weighed or otherwise measured;
(c)     the sample is secured in a container which is wrapped and sealed; and
(d)     the container is signed or initialled by a senior police officer and the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent.
(2)   A sample under subsection (1) shall be admitted as evidence in a criminal trial although the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent has refused to sign or initial the container containing the sample.
(3)   Where a sample of any substance is taken, the police shall issue a certificate in the form set out in Schedule 2.
(4)   Where the accused or the accused's attorney-at-law or agent has refused to sign or initial the container containing the sample the police shall issue a certificate, in the form set out in Schedule 3.
(5)   A copy of the certificate shall be served on the accused, the accused's attorney-at-law or agent, and the person executing such service shall make an endorsement of service on the original certificate.
(6)   In any proceedings where it is desired to admit a sample for use as evidence, the original police certificate is conclusive of facts stated in the certificate.
(7)   A police officer shall submit a sample which is to be used as evidence to an analyst for analysis or examination and where the analyst has made an analysis or examination, and the sample is not a sample of a controlled drug within the meaning of section 4 of the Drug (Prevention of Misuse) Act the analyst shall issue to the police officer a report stating—
(a)     the name of the police officer who submitted the sample;
(b)     the amount of the substance received;
(c)     the results of the examination or analysis.
(8)   Where the sample submitted for analysis under subsection (7) is a sample of a controlled drug within the meaning of section 4 of the Drugs (Prevention of Misuse) Act, the analyst shall issue to the police the certificate required under section 29 of that Act.
(9)   A copy of the analyst's report under subsection (7) shall be served on the accused, his or her attorney-at-law or agent personally or by registered post within 21 days of its being issued to the police.
(10)   Where an analyst's report has been issued, the Commissioner of Police shall as soon as may be practicable destroy or dispose of the bulk of any substance retained by the police.

5.   Modifications to Criminal Code
The Criminal Code shall be read with such modifications as are necessary to bring it into conformity with this Act.

Schedule 1
(Section 3(7))
UNDERTAKING BY OWNER OF PROPERTY SUBJECT TO INVESTIGATION
I       of
hereby undertake to make available to the Commissioner of Police, upon request for use at a criminal trial, the following good(s)/property:
(description of good(s)/property)
I understand that failure to produce such good(s)/property may render me liable to prosecution under the Criminal Code.
Dated this       day of       20
Owner
Witness

Schedule 2
(Section 4(3))
CERTIFICATE OF SAMPLE OF SEIZED SUBSTANCE
I HEREBY CERTIFY that on the       day of
20     , I took a sample of       from the bulk and
that the bulk was weighed or otherwise measured and the sample was taken and weighed or otherwise measured in the presence of
(Name)
(Address)
*Accused/*Attorney-at-law/*Agent
Measurement of bulk       g/kg/ml/l.
Measurement of sample       g/kg/ml/l.
Signature and Rank
Police Officer
Signature       *Accused
*Attorney-at-law
*Agent
(*
delete if inapplicable
)

Schedule 3
(Section 4(4))
CERTIFICATE OF SAMPLE OF SEIZED SUBSTANCE (SIGNATURE NOT PROVIDED)
I HEREBY CERTIFY that on the       day of
20     , I took a sample of:
(description of substance)
from the bulk and that the bulk was weighed or otherwise measured and the sample was taken and weighed or otherwise measured in the presence of
of
(Name)
(Address)
*Accused/*Attorney-at-law/*Agent
Measurement of bulk       g./kg/ml/l.
Measurement of sample       g./kg/ml/l.
I FURTHER CERTIFY that the *Accused/*Attorney-at-law/*Agent refused to sign or initial the container containing the sample.
Signature and Rank
Police Officer
* (
delete if inapplicable
)

SUBSIDIARY LEGISLATION
